replication_study.hypothesis: Branding alters attitude functions such that the Level (Category vs. Brand) × Unbranded Product Type (Utilitarian vs. Symbolic) interaction on the benefit–symbolism difference score (Benefits − Symbols) is statistically significant. Specifically, relative to Category, Brand: (a) reduces utilitarianism (lower difference score) for utilitarian product categories and (b) increases utilitarianism (higher difference score) for symbolic product categories.

data_plan.source_type: Online survey (Qualtrics) with randomized between-subject Level (Category vs Brand) and within-subject Product Type (Utilitarian vs Symbolic) across 8 products
data_plan.wave_or_subset: Four condition cells: Cond=1,2 (NoBrand/Category), Cond=3 (Brand A), Cond=4 (Brand B). Eight items (4 utilitarian, 4 symbolic) per respondent.
data_plan.sample_size: To be computed at runtime after preregistered exclusions; script reports retained N (see replication_results_summary.json).
data_plan.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant (collapsed to participant × ProductType × Level)
data_plan.access_details: Data and item maps are provided in /app/data at runtime. Original files located in data/original/23/input/replication_data.

qualification.explanation: This dataset replicates the original Study 1b design with independent respondents and the same operational measures (benefits and symbolism ratings) over the same set of product categories and branded variants. It is sufficiently different to test robustness (new sample/time) while maintaining high fidelity (same constructs and procedure).
qualification.similarity_to_original: Original paper (original_paper.pdf, Study 1b) used 8 products (4 utilitarian: paper towels, toothpaste, vitamins, cough syrup; 4 symbolic: college T-shirts, American flags, class rings, greeting cards) with Level (brand vs category) and measured benefits and symbolism, then tested a Level × Product Type mixed ANOVA (p. 351–353; F(1,204)=7.70, p=.006). The replication items metadata (ItemsList_Final.csv) lists the same categories and maps ItemCatID and Cond, including ProductType, ensuring the same product grouping. The focal claim and test are also recorded in initial_details.txt (Level × Product Type interaction) and corroborated by post_registration.json (Experimental; mixed ANOVAs on benefit vs symbolism difference scores).
qualification.deviation_from_original: While the original Study 1b sample size was N=206 (original_paper.pdf; post_registration.json), the replication is a new sample collected in 2020 (StartDate column in LeBeouf_replication_data.csv shows 11/3/20 entries), with preregistered exclusions applied (Status==0, Attention1==7, Attention2==1, screenQ==2) as specified in the provided R scripts (LeBoeuf_JournMarketRes_2010_EKBZ_Yoon_Direct Replication_618K_Final.R). The brands are handled as two branded sets (Cond 3 and 4) consistent with the original manipulation (Study 1b used two brand sets; original_paper.pdf, p. 351–352). Minor file-format deviations (ItemsList_Final.csv vs original R expecting .xlsx) are addressed in the Python rewrite.

data_plan.notes: Qualtrics export column names include repeated item blocks with suffixes like '1_Util...11', '1_Symbol...12' repeated 3 times. The Python code disambiguates by regex and column order. All file IO is confined to /app/data at runtime. ItemsList_Final.csv contains 32 rows mapping ItemCatID and Cond to Category, ProductType, and BrandType. The script merges by (ItemCatID, Cond).

planned_method.steps: 1) Read LeBeouf_replication_data.csv and ItemsList_Final.csv from /app/data. 2) Apply preregistered exclusions: Status==0; screenQ not NA and ==2; Attention1==7; Attention2==1. 3) For Cond in {1,2} use first item block; Cond==3 use second block; Cond==4 use third block. 4) Reshape to long by ItemCatID=1..8 with columns Benefits and Symbols. 5) Merge with ItemsList_Final.csv on (ItemCatID, Cond) to attach ProductType and Category. 6) Compute ScoreDiff = Benefits − Symbols. 7) Aggregate mean ScoreDiff by participant × ProductType × Condition (Category vs Brand). 8) Run a 2×2 mixed ANOVA: within=ProductType, between=Condition, subject=ID. 9) Save full ANOVA table and a JSON summary to /app/data.
planned_method.models: 2×2 mixed ANOVA (within: ProductType [Utilitarian vs Symbolic]; between: Condition [Category vs Brand]) on ScoreDiff.
planned_method.outcome_variable: ScoreDiff = Benefits − Symbols (per item; aggregated to within-subject ProductType and between-subject Condition).
planned_method.independent_variables: Condition (Category vs Brand; between-subject), ProductType (Utilitarian vs Symbolic; within-subject).
planned_method.control_variables: Stimulus item effects mitigated by within-subject aggregation over the four items per product type; order was counterbalanced by design (per original and items mapping).
planned_method.tools_software: Python 3.10; pandas, numpy, pingouin (for mixed_anova), scipy, statsmodels, matplotlib, seaborn, scikit-learn.

planned_estimation_and_test.estimation: F-statistic and partial eta-squared (np2) for the Level × ProductType interaction effect on ScoreDiff.
planned_estimation_and_test.test: Mixed ANOVA (between: Condition; within: ProductType) with p-value for the interaction (two-sided, alpha=0.05).

planned_method.missing_data_handling: Listwise deletion via numeric coercion (non-numeric ratings become NaN), reshape only on available blocks, and aggregation over available items; participants without both ProductType levels are excluded from the ANOVA.
planned_method.multiple_testing_policy: Not applicable; single focal interaction test. If any exploratory contrasts are run, no correction will be applied unless specified.
planned_method.inference_criteria: Primary criterion: significant Level × ProductType interaction (p < 0.05). Secondary: pattern consistent with branding reducing utilitarianism for utilitarian products and reducing symbolism (increasing utilitarian difference score) for symbolic products.

files.LeBoeuf_replication_analysis__py.py: Python translation of the provided R analysis pipeline. Reads replication CSV and items CSV from /app/data; applies preregistered exclusions; reshapes three item blocks by condition; merges items metadata; computes ScoreDiff; aggregates to participant × ProductType × Condition; runs 2×2 mixed ANOVA with pingouin; writes mixed_anova_results.csv and replication_results_summary.json to /app/data.

codebase.notes: All IO is hardcoded to /app/data as required. The script handles Qualtrics-style column names by regex and block order. Outputs: /app/data/mixed_anova_results.csv and /app/data/replication_results_summary.json. R scripts are preserved but not executed; Python rewrite is the entrypoint.

analysis.instructions: 1) Ensure LeBeouf_replication_data.csv and ItemsList_Final.csv are present in /app/data. 2) Run: python data/original/23/input/replication_data/LeBoeuf_replication_analysis__py.py. 3) Inspect outputs in /app/data: mixed_anova_results.csv (full ANOVA table) and replication_results_summary.json (interaction summary and notes).
analysis.comparison_metrics: Compare the Level × ProductType interaction F-statistic, p-value, and directionality to the original benchmark (Study 1b: F(1,204)=7.70, p=.006; original_paper.pdf; initial_details.txt). Optionally compare condition means (Category vs Brand) within each ProductType to confirm the predicted pattern.
